title: Tumor infilitrating cells engineered to express a pro-inflammatory polypeptide
abstract: The present disclosure provides methods of preparing tumor infiltrating cells engineered to express a pro-inflammatory polypeptide. The pro-inflammatory polypeptide is expressed from the tumor infiltrating cell to counter a generally immunosuppressive state in and around tumors resulting from an imbalance between the number and activation state of immune effector cells versus those of suppressor cells. Delivering the proinflammatory polypeptide via expression from the TICs, as distinct from systemic administration, reduces side effects from increased inflammation at sides remote from a tumor to be treated.
claims: Claims WHAT IS CLAIMED IS: 1. An isolated tumor-infiltrating cell genetically engineered to provide increased expression of a pro-inflammatory protein other than a chimeric antigen receptor (CAR). 2. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1, genetically engineered for expression of an exogenous nucleic acid encoding the pro-inflammatory protein. 3. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1, genetically engineered for expression of an exogenous nucleic acid encoding a transcriptional activator that increases expression of an endogenous copy of the pro-inflammatory protein. 4. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 2 or 3, further comprising a nucleic acid encoding a CAR. 5. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1 or 2, wherein the tumor-infiltrating cell is derived from a cell isolated from a solid tumor biopsy. 6. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1 or 2, wherein the tumor-infiltrating cell is derived from a cell isolated from peripheral blood or a lymphoid tissue. 7. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1 or 2, wherein the cell is a leukocyte. 8. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1 or 2, wherein the cell is a T cell, macrophage, or NK cell. 9. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1 or 2, wherein the cell expresses at least one marker selected from the group consisting of CD3, CD4, CD8, T-bet, GATA-3, CD25, Foxp3, ROR-gammaT, CD38, and CD40. 10. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1 or 2, wherein the cell expresses at least one marker selected from the group consisting of CD56, CD16, F480, siglec3, and gamma receptor. 11. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 2, wherein the pro-inflammatory protein encoded by the exogenous nucleic acid comprises a cytokine. 12. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 11, wherein the cytokine is selected from the group consisting of IL-2, IL-7, IL-15, IFN (type 1 or 2), CSF, GM-CSF, TNF-alpha, and IL-21. 13. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 2, wherein the pro-inflammatory protein encoded by the exogenous nucleic acid comprises an antibody. 14. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 13, wherein the antibody is selected from the group consisting of anti CTLA-4, anti-PD-1, and anti-PD-Ll. 15. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 2, wherein the pro-inflammatory protein encoded by the exogenous nucleic acid comprises a chemokine. 16. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 15, wherein the chemokine is selected from the group consisting of RANTES, IP-10, CXCL9, and CXCL10. 17. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 2, wherein the pro-inflammatory protein encoded by the exogenous nucleic acid comprises a microbial antigen. 18. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 17, wherein the microbial antigen is selected from the group consisting of SEA, SEA/E-120, and a bacterial flagellar protein. 19. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 2, wherein the pro-inflammatory protein is expressed as a fusion protein. 20. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1 or 2, wherein the exogenous nucleic acid is operably linked to an inducible promoter. 21. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 20, wherein the inducible promoter is a tetracycline-inducible promoter. 22. The isolated tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1 or 2, further comprising a nucleic acid encoding a matrix degrading enzyme. 23. The isolating tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 22, wherein the matrix degrading enzyme is selected from the group consisting of heparinase, collagenase, a matrix metalloproteinase, and plasminogen activator. 24. The isolating tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 22, wherein the matrix degrading enzyme is MMP9 or urokinase. 25. A method of preparing a tumor-infiltrating cell, the method comprising: a. obtaining a sample from a patient; b. isolating tumor-infiltrating cells from the sample; and c. transforming the tumor-infiltrating cells with an exogenous nucleic acid encoding a pro-inflammatory polypeptide other than a chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) or a transcription factor that induces expression of the pro-inflammatory polypeptide. 26. The method of claim 25, wherein the sample comprises a tumor biopsy or a fine needle aspirate (FNA). 27. The method of claim 25, wherein the sample comprises peripheral blood or a lymphoid tissue biopsy. 28. The method of claim 25, wherein the step of isolating tumor-infiltrating cells from the sample comprises dissociating the sample into single cells. 29. The method of claim 25, wherein the tumor-infiltrating cells are tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs). 30. The method of claim 29, wherein the TILs are cytotoxic T cells or T helper cells. 31. The method of claim 25 or 29, wherein the step of isolating the tumor-infiltrating cells comprises contacting the sample, or cells dissociated therefrom, with an agent that specifically binds to a cell type-specific cell surface marker. 32. The method of claim 31, wherein the agent is an anti-CD4 and/or anti-CD8 antibody. 33. The method of claim 31, wherein the agent is attached to a solid support. 34. The method of claim 25 or 29, wherein the step of isolating tumor-infiltrating cells comprises performing FACS or MACS. a. The method of claim 25 or 29, further comprising: b. introducing the isolated tumor-infiltrating cells into a microfluidic device; and c. placing one or more isolated tumor-infiltrating cells into each of a plurality of isolation chambers located within the microfluidic device. 35. The method of claim 35, wherein the microfluidic device is a nanofluidic device. 36. The method of claim 35, wherein the step of placing the one or more isolated tumor- infiltrating cells into isolation chambers comprises using an electrokinetic force to move the cells into the plurality of isolation chambers. 37. The method of claim 37, wherein the electrokinetic force is a dielectrophoretic force. 38. The method of claims 35, wherein the steps of introducing and placing the one or more isolated tumor-infiltration cells are performed after the step of transforming the tumor- infiltrating cells. 39. The method of claim 25 or 29, wherein the step of transforming the tumor-infiltrating cells comprises transforming the cells with a viral vector. 40. The method of claim 40, wherein the viral vector is a lentiviral vector. 41. The method of claim 25 or 29, wherein the step of transforming the tumor-infiltrating cells comprises introducing CRISPR-Cas9, a TALEN, or a zinc finger protein into the cells. 42. The method of claim 35, further comprising culturing the transformed cells within the isolation chambers of the microfluidic device. 43. The method of claim 43, wherein the transformed cells are cultured individually in separate isolation chambers to thereby produce clonal populations of transformed cells. 44. The method of claim 25 or 29, further comprising culturing the transformed cells in the presence of rapamycin. 45. The method of claim 25 or 29, further comprising administering a transformed cell to a patient. 46. The method of claim 46, wherein the patient receiving the transformed cell is the same patient from whom the sample was obtained. 47. A method of treating a patient having a cancer, the method comprising administering to the patient an effective regime of a genetically engineered tumor-infiltrating cell of claim 1 or 2. 48. The method of claim 48, wherein the cancer is a melanoma, a breast cancer, a genitourinary cancer, a cancer of the nervous system, an intestinal cancer, or a lung cancer. 49. The method of claim 48, wherein the genetically engineered tumor-infiltrating cells comprise an exogenous nucleic acid operably linked to an inducible promoter, and wherein the method further comprises administering to the patient an agent capable of inducing expression of the exogenous nucleic acid. 50. The method of claim 50, wherein the exogenous nucleic acid comprises a tetracycline- inducible promoter and the agent administered to the patient is tetracycline. 51. The method of claim 48, wherein at least 10 3 genetically engineered tumor-infiltrating cells are administered to the patient. 52. The method of claim 48, wherein 10 3 -10 5 genetically engineered tumor-infiltrating cells are administered to the patient. 53. The method of claim 52, wherein the genetically engineered tumor-infiltrating cells administered to the patient comprise a mixture of genetically engineered tumor-infiltrating cell populations, each population clonally derived from a single genetically engineered tumor- infiltrating cell. 54. The method of claim 54, wherein at least 95% of the cells in the mixture are from one of the clonal populations of genetically engineered tumor-infiltrating cells. 55. A composition comprising a mixture of genetically engineered tumor-infiltrating cell populations, each population clonally derived from a single genetically engineered tumor- infiltrating cell of claim 1 or 2. 56. The composition of claim 56, containing substantially no other cells than the cells from one of the clonal populations of genetically engineered tumor-infiltrating cells. 57. The composition of claim 56, wherein at least 95% of the cells in the mixture are from one of the clonal populations of genetically engineered tumor-infiltrating cells. 58. The composition of claim 56, wherein the composition comprises at least 10 4 cells. 59. The composition of claim 56 further comprising a pharmaceutically acceptable carrier.
